sets.title: Passé Composé vs Imparfait
sets.description: Passé composé presents past events as completed and countable; imparfait presents them as ongoing, habitual, or as background. The distinction is about viewpoint, not distance in time.

pairs.sentence_a: Il pleuvait quand je suis sorti.
pairs.sentence_b: Il a plu et je suis sorti.
pairs.question: Which sentence says it was already raining when you stepped out?
pairs.explanation: "Pleuvait" (imparfait) sets the weather as a continuous background — it was already raining, and into that scene you stepped out. "A plu" (passé composé) makes the rain a discrete event in sequence before leaving. Imparfait = backdrop; passé composé = plot point.

pairs.sentence_a: Quand j'étais enfant, je lisais beaucoup.
pairs.sentence_b: Hier, j'ai lu un roman entier.
pairs.question: Which sentence describes a specific day's accomplishment?
pairs.explanation: "Lisais" (imparfait) describes a habit across an indefinite stretch of childhood — no single event, just a pattern. "Ai lu" (passé composé) pins a concrete completion to yesterday — one book, one day, fully read.

sets.title: Subjonctif vs Indicatif
sets.description: The indicatif reports facts and expectations; the subjonctif encodes doubt, wish, necessity, or attitude. The governing verb or expression determines which mood follows.

pairs.sentence_b: Je ne pense pas qu'il vienne.
pairs.question: Which sentence expresses doubt about his coming?
pairs.explanation: "Vient" (indicatif) follows an affirmative penser and reports a likely fact. "Vienne" (subjonctif) follows a negated penser — doubt is introduced, and French marks that doubt morphologically. Negating a verb of thought often shifts its complement into subjonctif.

pairs.question: Which sentence states a fact about her departure?
pairs.explanation: "Parte" (subjonctif) follows il faut — necessity does not assert the event as real, only as required. "Part" (indicatif) follows il est certain — certainty asserts the event as real. Subjonctif = world as it should be; indicatif = world as it is.
